items.title: Unpacking Augmented Marauder’s Multi-Pronged Casbaneiro Campaigns
items.description: BlueVoyant researchers have uncovered a broad, multi-pronged phishing campaign targeting Spanish-speaking users in organizations across Latin America and now Europe as well. While recent industry intelligence heavily documented attacks utilizing WhatsApp to deliver banking trojans under the umbrella of the Brazil-based eCrime group Augmented Marauder (a.k.a. Water Saci)
items.ai_summary: The Augmented Marauder group is conducting a phishing campaign targeting Spanish-speaking users, utilizing WhatsApp to deliver banking trojans. This campaign has real-world impact as it can lead to financial loss and sensitive information theft. The most important defender action is to educate users about phishing attacks and implement robust email and messaging filters.

graph LR

items.title: From Inbox to Intrusion: Multi‑Stage Remcos RAT and C2‑Delivered Payloads in Network
items.description: This multi-stage fileless Remcos RAT attack leverages a phishing-delivered JavaScript dropper to trigger a reflective PowerShell loader that executes payloads entirely in memory. The infection chain utilizes obfuscation techniques like rotational XOR and Base64 encoding to reconstruct .NET payloads, significantly reducing the disk-based detection footprint. Stealth is maintained by using aspnet_co
items.ai_summary: This threat involves a multi-stage Remcos RAT attack delivered via phishing, utilizing JavaScript and PowerShell to execute payloads in memory. The attack leverages obfuscation techniques to evade detection, and defenders should prioritize monitoring for suspicious PowerShell activity. To defend against this threat, focus on implementing robust email filtering and user education to prevent initial phishing attacks.

graph LR

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Persistence]:::tactic

items.title: Funnull Resurfaces: Exposing RingH23 Arsenal and MacCMS Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: The report details the resurgence of the Funnull cybercriminal group, now utilizing a new arsenal called RingH23. It exposes their tactics, including compromising GoEdge CDN nodes, poisoning the MacCMS supply chain, and deploying sophisticated malware components like Badredis2s, Badnginx2s, and Badhide2s. The group has expanded its operations to inject malicious JavaScript, hijack cryptocurrency t
items.ai_summary: The Funnull group has resurfaced with a new arsenal called RingH23, compromising GoEdge CDN nodes and poisoning the MacCMS supply chain through tactics like JavaScript injection. This resurgence poses a significant threat due to the group's sophisticated malware components. To defend against this, it's crucial to monitor supply chain vulnerabilities and implement robust JavaScript validation.

items.title: Inside the Axios supply chain compromise - one RAT to rule them all
items.description: Elastic Security Labs identified a supply chain compromise of the axios npm package, one of the most depended-upon packages in the JavaScript ecosystem with approximately 100 million weekly downloads. The attacker compromised a maintainer account and published backdoored versions that delivered a cross-platform Remote Access Trojan to macOS, Windows, and Linux systems through a malicious postinsta
items.ai_summary: The threat is a supply chain compromise of the axios npm package, delivering a cross-platform Remote Access Trojan to systems through a malicious postinstall script. This has a significant real-world impact due to the package's widespread use, with approximately 100 million weekly downloads. The most important defender action is to verify the integrity of axios packages and monitor for suspicious activity.

items.title: Latest Xloader Obfuscation Methods and Network Protocol
items.description: Xloader is an information stealing malware family that evolved from Formbook and targets web browsers, email clients, and File Transfer Protocol (FTP) applications. Additionally, Xloader may execute arbitrary commands and download second-stage payloads on an infected system. The author of Xloader continues to update the codebase, with the most recent observed version being 8.7. Since version 8.1,
items.ai_summary: Xloader is an information stealing malware that targets web browsers, email clients, and FTP applications, with capabilities to execute arbitrary commands and download second-stage payloads. Its latest versions have incorporated new obfuscation methods, making it challenging to detect. To defend against Xloader, it is essential to implement robust endpoint detection and response systems to identify and mitigate potential threats.

items.title: North Korea-Nexus Threat Actor Compromises Widely Used Axios NPM Package in Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) is tracking an active software supply chain attack targeting the popular Node Package Manager (NPM) package "axios." Between March 31, 2026, 00:21 and 03:20 UTC, an attacker introduced a malicious dependency named "plain-crypto-js" into axios NPM releases versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4. Axios is the most popular JavaScript library used to simplify HTTP requests,
items.ai_summary: The North Korea-nexus threat actor compromised the widely used Axios NPM package by introducing a malicious dependency, allowing for supply chain attacks. This attack has a significant real-world impact as Axios is a popular JavaScript library used for HTTP requests. The most important defender action is to immediately update or patch vulnerable Axios versions to prevent further compromise.

graph LR

items.title: Stranger Strings: Yurei Ransomware Operator Toolkit Exposed
items.description: Active since September 2025, Yurei is a double extortion ransomware campaign. The operators run their own Tor data leak site with a low number of victims listed at the time of writing. It is reportedly derived from Prince Ransomware, an open-source ransomware family written in Go. Check Point researchers noted that all samples were first submitted to VirusTotal from Morocco, and that one sample di
items.ai_summary: Yurei ransomware is a double extortion campaign derived from Prince Ransomware, utilizing public-facing application exploits and data exfiltration to encrypt data for impact. The real-world impact includes data breaches and financial losses for affected organizations. The most important defender action is to ensure robust security measures are in place to prevent public-facing application exploits.

items.title: Cisco Talos: Qilin EDR killer infection chain
items.description: Endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools are widely deployed and far more capable than traditional antivirus. As a result, attackers use EDR killers to disable or bypass them. The malicious “msimg32.dll” used in Qilin ransomware attacks, which is a multi-stage infection chain targeting EDR systems. It can terminate over 300 different EDR drivers from almost every vendor in the market.
items.ai_summary: The Qilin EDR killer is a multi-stage infection chain that targets endpoint detection and response systems, using a malicious 'msimg32.dll' to terminate over 300 EDR drivers. This allows attackers to bypass security measures and encrypt data for ransom. To defend against this threat, organizations should prioritize implementing robust EDR solutions and regularly updating their security software.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1104| B[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    B -->|T1486| C[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Blurred Lines: AdTech Abuse Delivers Browser Hijackers Through the Microsoft Store
items.description: A newly uncovered campaign abuses the Trillion (formerly Trellian) AdTech network, mimicking the flow of a Traffic Direction System (TDS) to trick visitors of typo-squatted domains into downloading Microsoft Store apps that contain browser hijacking malware. While the abuse of AdTech networks to deliver malware isn’t new, this campaign highlights incredibly similar tactics to VexTrio and previous
items.ai_summary: The threat involves abusing the Trillion AdTech network to deliver browser hijackers through the Microsoft Store, leveraging typo-squatted domains to trick users into downloading malicious apps. This campaign showcases tactics similar to VexTrio, highlighting the ongoing abuse of AdTech networks for malware delivery. Defenders should prioritize monitoring and filtering of Microsoft Store apps to prevent such infections.

items.title: Signed malware impersonating workplace apps deploys RMM backdoors
items.description: Multiple phishing campaigns were identified using workplace meeting lures, PDF attachments, and abuse of legitimate binaries to deliver signed malware. The attacks used digitally signed executables masquerading as legitimate software to install remote monitoring and management (RMM) tools like ScreenConnect, Tactical RMM, and Mesh Agent. These tools enabled attackers to establish persistence and m
items.ai_summary: The threat involves signed malware masquerading as legitimate workplace software, delivered through phishing campaigns, to deploy RMM backdoors. This allows attackers to establish persistence and gain remote access to compromised systems. Defenders should prioritize verifying the authenticity of software and attachments to prevent initial access.

items.title: CVE-2019-25635: Zeeways Matrimony CMS contains multiple SQL injection vulnerabilities that allow...
items.description: Zeeways Matrimony CMS contains multiple SQL injection vulnerabilities that allow unauthenticated attackers to manipulate database queries through the profile_list endpoint. Attackers can inject SQL code via the up_cast, s_mother, and s_religion parameters to extract sensitive database information using time-based or error-based techniques.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2019-25635 is a SQL injection vulnerability in Zeeways Matrimony CMS, allowing unauthenticated attackers to manipulate database queries through the profile_list endpoint. This vulnerability can be exploited using time-based or error-based techniques, potentially leading to sensitive database information extraction. To defend against this threat, prioritize input validation and sanitization for all user-supplied data.

items.title: CVE-2019-25634: Base64 Decoder 1.1.2 contains a stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability that a...
items.description: Base64 Decoder 1.1.2 contains a stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability that allows local attackers to execute arbitrary code by triggering a structured exception handler (SEH) overwrite. Attackers can craft a malicious input file that overflows a buffer, overwrites the SEH chain with a POP-POP-RET gadget address, and uses an egghunter payload to locate and execute shellcode for code execution.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2019-25634 is a stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in Base64 Decoder 1.1.2 that allows local attackers to execute arbitrary code. This vulnerability can be exploited by crafting a malicious input file that overflows a buffer and overwrites the SEH chain. To defend against this threat, prioritize patching vulnerable systems and implementing exploit mitigation techniques such as address space layout randomization (ASLR) and data execution prevention (DEP).

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

Attackers can inject crafted payloads into the Display name field and Load from file parameter to trigger the overflow and execute shellcode with application pri
items.ai_summary: CVE-2019-25633 is a buffer overflow vulnerability in AIDA64 Extreme 5.99.4900, allowing local attackers to execute arbitrary code through malicious input in the email preferences and report wizard interfaces. This vulnerability can lead to code execution with application privileges, posing a significant threat to system security. To defend against this threat, users should prioritize updating AIDA64 Extreme to a patched version.

items.title: CVE-2019-25632: phpFileManager 1.7.8 contains a local file inclusion vulnerability that allows u...
items.description: phpFileManager 1.7.8 contains a local file inclusion vulnerability that allows unauthenticated attackers to read arbitrary files by manipulating the action, fm_current_dir, and filename parameters. Attackers can send GET requests to index.php with crafted parameter values to access sensitive files like /etc/passwd from the server.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2019-25632 is a local file inclusion vulnerability in phpFileManager 1.7.8, allowing unauthenticated attackers to read arbitrary files by manipulating parameters in GET requests to index.php. This can lead to exposure of sensitive files like /etc/passwd. To defend, patch or update phpFileManager to a version that fixes this vulnerability.

items.title: CVE-2019-25631: AIDA64 Business 5.99.4900 contains a structured exception handling buffer overfl...
items.description: AIDA64 Business 5.99.4900 contains a structured exception handling buffer overflow vulnerability that allows local attackers to execute arbitrary code by overwriting SEH pointers with malicious shellcode. Attackers can inject egg hunter shellcode through the SMTP display name field in preferences or report wizard functionality to trigger the overflow and execute code with application privileges.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2019-25631 is a structured exception handling buffer overflow vulnerability in AIDA64 Business 5.99.4900, allowing local attackers to execute arbitrary code with application privileges. The vulnerability can be triggered by injecting malicious shellcode through the SMTP display name field. To defend, prioritize patching the vulnerable AIDA64 Business version to prevent exploitation.

items.description: PhreeBooks ERP 5.2.3 contains an arbitrary file upload vulnerability in the Image Manager component that allows authenticated attackers to upload malicious files by submitting requests to the image upload endpoint. Attackers can upload PHP files through the imgFile parameter to the bizuno/image/manager endpoint and execute them via the bizunoFS.php script for remote code execution.

items.title: DShield (Cowrie) Honeypot Stats and When Sessions Disconnect, (Mon, Mar 30th)
items.description: A lot of the information seen on DShield honeypots [1] is repeated bot traffic, especially when looking at the Cowrie [2] telnet and SSH sessions. However, how long a session lasts, how many commands are run per session and what the last commands run before a session disconnects can vary. Some of this information could help indicate whether a session is automated and if a honeypot was fingerprinted. This information can also be used to find more interesting honeypot sessions.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.

items.title: Kimwolf Botnet Swamps Anonymity Network I2P
items.description: For the past week, the massive "Internet of Things" (IoT) botnet known as Kimwolf has been disrupting the The Invisible Internet Project (I2P), a decentralized, encrypted communications network designed to anonymize and secure online communications. I2P users started reporting disruptions in the network around the same time the Kimwolf botmasters began relying on it to evade takedown attempts against the botnet's control servers.
